Full statement: microwaves travel through air at just slightly below speed of light, whereas signals travel through fiber-optic channels and copper wires only two-thirds as quickly; also minimize number of electronic routers and switches through which communications pass (each adds latency).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic traders and brokers also use the fastest communication technologies they can obtain to collect and transmit information when any distance separates the places where information events occur from the places where they act on those events. To that end, they use the fastest and most direct communication lines that are available. For example, they prefer line-of-sight microwave channels to fiber-optic and copper channels because of the differences in speed of electromagnetic wave propagation through these materials. (Microwaves travel through air at just slightly below the speed of light, whereas signals travel through fiber-optic channels and copper wires only two-thirds as quickly.) They also ensure that their communications pass through the fewest electronic routers and switches possible because passage through each of these devices adds its latency to the total latency of the line.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Microwaves through AIR travel just under lightspeed; signals in fiber and copper propagate meaningfully slower.

options.A: Electronic traders pursuing fast computations rely on deliberately slowed, underclocked processors, accepting lower speeds in exchange for the thermal stability that continuous trading requires.
options.B: Fast computation for electronic trading requires no hardware or software optimization, since exchange matching engines impose uniform processing delays that equalize all participants.
options.C: very fast overclocked processors (with liquid cooling), fast memory (avoiding physical disk drive latencies), specialized processors (sometimes on gallium arsenide vs silicon), simple efficient operating systems (variants of MS-DOS due to simplicity), assembler language for repeated functions, avoiding interpreter languages like Python for time-critical code.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The direction is the opposite — OVERCLOCKED processors with liquid cooling.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Optimization is comprehensive: fast memory avoiding disk latency, specialized processors, streamlined code.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'First and most obviously, they use very fast computers. They overclock their processors (i.e., run them faster than the processor designers intended) and use liquid cooling systems to keep them from melting. They store all information in fast memory to avoid the latencies associated with physical disk drives... They sometimes use specialized processors designed to solve their specific trading problems quickly, and they may even use processors etched on gallium arsenide rather than silicon. Electronic traders also must run very efficient software. They often use simple and specialized operating systems to avoid the overhead associated with supporting operating system functions they do not use. Remarkably, many electronic trading systems run under variants of the original MS-DOS operating system because of its simplicity. Electronic traders optimize their computer code for speed. They often write important functions that they repeatedly use in assembler language to ensure that they run quickly.'

options.A: contain prearranged action plans — traders reduce their decision latencies by doing analyses before events instead of afterward.
options.B: Contingency tables waste electronic traders' time by requiring analysis of events that may never occur.
options.C: Contingency tables serve no purpose in latency-sensitive trading.

rationales.A:
Full statement: contain prearranged action plans — traders reduce their decision latencies by doing analyses before events instead of afterward; seeing an event (e.g., bid increase), they respond by simply looking up optimal response in a contingency table stored in memory; must be kept up to date and include responses for most-likely events.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Some electronic traders also reduce latency by creating contingency tables that contain prearranged action plans. For example, suppose that a bid rises in a market in which electronic traders are active. In response to the increased bid, traders may want to raise their bids or offers. The decision to do so may depend on their inventory positions and perhaps on many other factors as well. To decide what to do following an increased bid may require substantial analyses, which take time. Traders can reduce their decision latencies by doing these analyses before the bid increases instead of afterward. Seeing the increased bid, they can respond by simply looking up the optimal response in a contingency table stored in memory. To be most useful, the contingency tables must be kept up to date and must include responses for most-likely events.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Pre-computation is the SAVINGS — analysis done before events instead of after.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The purpose is decision-latency reduction: prearranged action plans triggered instantly.

options.A: The Knight Capital failure of 1 August 2012 was a minor incident with limited financial consequence.
options.B: was a runaway algorithm — due to a software programming mistake, Knight sent millions of orders to markets over 45-minute period when it intended only 212 orders.
options.C: The Knight Capital failure was caused by a human trader's manual order entry error.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The event was near-fatal to the firm — hundreds of millions lost in under an hour.
rationales.B:
Full statement: was a runaway algorithm — due to a software programming mistake, Knight sent millions of orders to markets over 45-minute period when it intended only 212 orders; produced 4 million executions involving 397 stocks; Knight lost $400 million.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Runaway algorithms produce streams of unintended orders that result from programming mistakes. The problems sometimes occur when programmers do not anticipate some contingency. The Knight Capital trading failure on 1 August 2012 may be the most extreme example of a runaway algorithm incident. Owing to a software programming mistake, Knight sent millions of orders to the markets over a 45-minute period when it intended only to fill 212 orders, some of which normally might have been broken up but none of which would have generated so many orders. These orders produced 4 million executions involving 397 stocks. Knight lost $400 million in the incident.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The cause was SOFTWARE — a programming mistake sent millions of unintended orders over 45 minutes.

options.A: The 6 May 2010 Flash Crash was not tied to any single identifiable order.
options.B: The Flash Crash was a slow, orderly decline spread evenly across the session.
options.C: was triggered by a large institutional trader (Waddell & Reed) selling $4.1B in E-mini S&P 500 futures using an algorithm designed to participate in fixed fraction of market volume.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Investigation traced a specific trigger — a $4.1B E-mini S&P 500 futures sale executed by algorithm.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The crash was FAST — a plunge and recovery within minutes, not a slow drift.
rationales.C:
Full statement: was triggered by a large institutional trader (Waddell & Reed) selling $4.1B in E-mini S&P 500 futures using an algorithm designed to participate in fixed fraction of market volume; initial trades depressed S&P 500 futures prices, trading volumes increased as arbitrageurs traded, causing algorithm to increase order submissions, exacerbating problem; market recovered after CME briefly halted E-mini futures trading; E-mini dropped ~5% in 5 minutes then recovered nearly fully.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Overlarge orders demand more liquidity than the market can provide. In these events, a trader — often inexperienced — will try to execute a marketable order that is too large for the market to handle without severely disrupting prices in the time given to fill the order. The 6 May 2010 Flash Crash occurred as a result of such an order. The crash was triggered when a large institutional trader tried to sell $4.1 billion in E-mini S&P 500 futures contracts using an algorithm over a short period. The algorithm was designed to participate in a fixed fraction of the market volume. When the initial trades depressed S&P 500 futures prices, trading volumes increased substantially as arbitrageurs and others started to trade. The increase in trading volumes caused the algorithm to increase the rate of its order submissions, which exacerbated the problem. The market reverted to its former levels after the Chicago Mercantile Exchange briefly halted trading in the E-mini S&P 500 futures contract, and the large order eventually was filled.'

options.A: the dissemination of false information about fundamental values or about other traders' trading intentions to alter investors' value assessments.
options.B: Rumormongering is always legal because opinions about securities are protected speech.
options.C: Rumormongering is not a practice recognized in market-abuse taxonomies.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the dissemination of false information about fundamental values or about other traders' trading intentions to alter investors' value assessments; illegal in most jurisdictions; analysts must ensure they base analyses on valid, balanced information, not false or one-sided information designed to distort analyses.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Rumormongering is the dissemination of false information about fundamental values or about other traders' trading intentions to alter investors' value assessments. Financial analysts must be careful to ensure that they base their analyses on valid information and not on false information designed to fool them into making poor decisions. Note that although rumormongering is illegal in most jurisdictions, simply reporting one side of an issue is not illegal. Financial analysts, therefore, must also be careful to ensure that they base their analyses on balanced information and not on information that is true but selectively presented to them with the purpose of distorting their analyses.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Disseminating FALSE information to move valuations is market manipulation, not protected opinion.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The practice is a recognized abuse — false claims about values or other traders' intentions.

options.A: Spoofing is a hidden-order strategy that conceals trading intentions from the market.
options.B: Spoofing is not a recognized practice in electronic markets.
options.C: traders placing exposed standing limit orders to convey impression to other traders that the market is more liquid than it is or to suggest security is under- or overvalued.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Spoofing uses EXPOSED standing limit orders — visibility is the mechanism of deception.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Spoofing/layering is a recognized and prosecuted abuse: fake displayed liquidity to mislead other traders.
rationales.C:
Full statement: traders placing exposed standing limit orders to convey impression to other traders that the market is more liquid than it is or to suggest security is under- or overvalued; example: spoofer places hidden buy order + exposed sell limit orders to convey price may fall; some traders sell into spoofer's buy order; spoofer then cancels sell orders.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Spoofing, also known as layering, is a trading practice in which traders place exposed standing limit orders to convey an impression to other traders that the market is more liquid than it is or to suggest to other traders that the security is under- or overvalued. For example, suppose that a spoofer wants to buy stock cheaply or quickly. The spoofer might place a hidden buy order in the market. The spoofer then places one or more exposed sell limit orders in the market to convey the impression that prices may soon fall. Seeing the spoofing sell orders, one or more traders may conclude that values may be lower than market prices suggest. On that basis, they may sell into the spoofer's buy order, enabling the spoofer to obtain a quick and possibly cheaper purchase.'

options.A: Gunning the market is not a recognized manipulation strategy.
options.B: strategy used by market manipulators to force traders to do disadvantageous trades — generally by selling quickly to push prices down with hope of triggering stop-loss sell orders.
options.C: Gunning the market is a legitimate strategy for providing liquidity in stressed conditions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The tactic is recognized manipulation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: strategy used by market manipulators to force traders to do disadvantageous trades — generally by selling quickly to push prices down with hope of triggering stop-loss sell orders; e.g., manipulator sells aggressively to push prices from 51 to 50 to trigger stop-loss sells clustered at 50, then repurchases at lower prices.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Gunning the market. Gunning the market is a strategy used by market manipulators to force traders to do disadvantageous trades. A manipulator generally guns the market by selling quickly to push prices down with the hope of triggering stop-loss sell orders. A stop-loss (or stop) sell order becomes valid for execution once the specified stop price condition is met by a trade occurring at or below the stop price. For example, suppose that a market manipulator believes that traders have placed many stop-loss sell orders at 50. These sell orders would become valid upon a trade occurring at 50 or below. The manipulator may sell aggressively to push prices down from 51 to 50 and thereby trigger the stop-loss sell orders. The manipulator then may be able to profit by repurchasing at lower prices.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Forcing disadvantageous trades — selling fast to push prices through stop levels — is abuse, not liquidity provision.

options.A: Squeezing and cornering are not practices that occur in modern markets, having been eliminated by position limits and electronic surveillance of deliverable contracts.
options.B: Squeezing and cornering are always legal expressions of market power, protected as legitimate accumulation of scarce assets by traders with superior foresight.
options.C: schemes to force traders to do disadvantageous trades — manipulator obtains control over resources necessary to settle trading contracts, then unexpectedly withdraws those resources from market causing traders to default on contracts (some of which manipulator may hold).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Both schemes persist, particularly around deliverable contracts.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Controlling the resources needed to settle contracts in order to force disadvantageous trades is illegal manipulation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: schemes to force traders to do disadvantageous trades — manipulator obtains control over resources necessary to settle trading contracts, then unexpectedly withdraws those resources from market causing traders to default on contracts (some of which manipulator may hold); short squeezes: manipulators control substantial fraction of lendable shares, then recall stock loans; commodity corners: manipulators buy many futures contracts + deliverable supply in spot; illegal in most jurisdictions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Squeezing and cornering. Squeezing, cornering, and gunning the market are all schemes that market manipulators use to force traders to do disadvantageous trades. In a squeeze or corner, the manipulator obtains control over resources necessary to settle trading contracts. The manipulator then unexpectedly withdraws those resources from the market, which causes traders to default on their contracts, some of which the manipulator may hold... For example, in short squeezes, manipulators obtain control of a substantial fraction of all available lendable stock shares or bonds. If the securities are overvalued... many speculators may be short selling the securities by unknowingly borrowing them from the manipulators. The manipulators then will recall the security loans. If the short sellers ("shorts") cannot borrow the securities from others, they will be forced to buy securities in the market to cover their stock loans... In commodity market corners, manipulators buy many futures contracts while simultaneously buying in the spot markets much of the deliverable supply of the commodity. Corners are illegal in most jurisdictions.'

options.A: Performance measurement draws no distinction between absolute return and excess return.
options.B: investment return tells us what the portfolio achieved over a specific period irrespective of peer or benchmark performance (absolute return).
options.C: Absolute and excess return are defined but never both used in evaluating the same portfolio.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The distinction is foundational — what the portfolio achieved versus what it achieved RELATIVE to a benchmark.
rationales.B:
Full statement: investment return tells us what the portfolio achieved over a specific period irrespective of peer or benchmark performance (absolute return); performance measurement also provides basis to understand difference between portfolio return and its benchmark return (excess return); this is different from 'excess return' used in Sharpe ratio context (return above risk-free rate).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The investment return tells us what the portfolio achieved over a specific period, irrespective of peer or benchmark performance. For purposes of this reading, we will call this the absolute return. But it also provides the basis to understand the difference between the portfolio return and its benchmark return, the excess return.' Note the distinction from Sharpe context: 'this additional return is often referred to as excess return. This use contrasts with how "excess return" is used in return performance attribution — that is, as a return in excess of a benchmark's return.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Both views are routinely applied together; each answers a different evaluation question.

options.A: The bottom-up stock picker example shows that sector attribution always aligns with any manager's process.
options.B: if portfolio manager is a genuine bottom-up stock picker who ignores sector benchmark weights, then measuring the impact of sector allocation against these weights is not measuring decisions made as part of the investment process.
options.C: No example connects attribution design to the manager's investment process.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The example shows the OPPOSITE — sector effects for a benchmark-agnostic stock picker measure a byproduct, not a decision.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if portfolio manager is a genuine bottom-up stock picker who ignores sector benchmark weights, then measuring the impact of sector allocation against these weights is not measuring decisions made as part of the investment process; sector effects are merely a byproduct of the manager's investment decisions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, if the portfolio manager is a genuine bottom-up stock picker who ignores sector benchmark weights, then measuring the impact of sector allocation against these weights is not measuring decisions made as part of the investment process; sector effects are merely a byproduct of the manager's investment decisions.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The stock-picker illustration exists precisely to make the process-alignment point.

options.A: Attribution has no use as a diagnostic or management tool beyond client reporting.
options.B: Attribution serves no purpose within the investment organization.
options.C: used by senior management, client relationship specialists, risk controllers, operations staff, portfolio managers, and sales/marketing professionals.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Diagnosis of where returns come from is attribution's core internal use.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Senior management, relationship specialists, risk controllers, operations, PMs, and sales all consume attribution output.
rationales.C:
Full statement: used by senior management, client relationship specialists, risk controllers, operations staff, portfolio managers, and sales/marketing professionals; provides insights to investment decision-making process; when it accurately reflects the investment decision-making process, return attribution provides quality control for the investment process and provides senior management with a tool to manage a complex business with multiple investment strategies; especially important when performance is weak (portfolio managers must demonstrate understanding of their performance).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Performance attribution is a critical component of the portfolio evaluation process. Used by senior management, client relationship specialists, risk controllers, operations staff, portfolio managers, and sales and marketing professionals, attribution analysis provides important insights to the investment decision-making process... Return attribution analysis is particularly important when performance is weak; portfolio managers must demonstrate an understanding of their performance, provide a rationale for their decisions, and generate confidence in their ability to add value in the future. When it accurately reflects the investment decision-making process, return attribution provides quality control for the investment process and provides senior management with a tool to manage a complex business with multiple investment strategies.'

options.A: performance attribution includes both return attribution and risk attribution (although in practice, 'performance attribution' is often used to mean 'return attribution').
options.B: Return attribution and risk attribution are the same analysis under two names.
options.C: No distinction exists between attributing returns and attributing risk.

rationales.A:
Full statement: performance attribution includes both return attribution and risk attribution (although in practice, 'performance attribution' is often used to mean 'return attribution'); return attribution analyzes impact of active investment decisions on returns; risk attribution analyzes the risk consequences of those decisions; depending on purpose of analysis, risk may be viewed in absolute or benchmark-relative terms.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Performance attribution includes return attribution and risk attribution (although in practice, "performance attribution" is often used to mean "return attribution"). Return attribution analyzes the impact of active investment decisions on returns; risk attribution analyzes the risk consequences of those decisions. Depending on the purpose of the analysis, risk may be viewed in absolute or benchmark-relative terms. For example, when risk relative to a benchmark is the focus, a risk attribution analysis might identify and evaluate a portfolio's deviations from a benchmark's exposures to risk factors.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. They decompose different quantities — realized return sources versus risk exposures.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The distinction is drawn explicitly, with 'performance attribution' colloquially meaning the return side.

options.A: Macro attribution has no defined framework in performance evaluation, since sponsor-level decisions cannot be decomposed with the same rigor as manager-level decisions.
options.B: measures the effect of the sponsor's choice to deviate from the strategic asset allocation, including effect of 'gaps' between strategic asset allocation and its implementation (e.g., where sum of the managers' benchmarks is equal to something other than the benchmark index).
options.C: Macro attribution and micro attribution are indistinguishable analyses, both measuring the individual manager's security selection within the assigned mandate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The framework is defined at the SPONSOR level.
rationales.B:
Full statement: measures the effect of the sponsor's choice to deviate from the strategic asset allocation, including effect of 'gaps' between strategic asset allocation and its implementation (e.g., where sum of the managers' benchmarks is equal to something other than the benchmark index); attribution used to evaluate the asset owner's tactical asset allocation and manager selection decisions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'attribution can also be conducted to evaluate the asset owner's tactical asset allocation and manager selection decisions (called macro attribution) or to evaluate the impact of the portfolio manager's decisions on the performance of the asset owner's total fund (called micro attribution). A defined-benefit pension plan makes the decision to allocate a given percentage of the fund to each asset class and decides which manager(s) to hire for each asset class. Macro attribution measures the effect of the sponsor's choice to deviate from the strategic asset allocation, including the effect of "gaps" between the strategic asset allocation and its implementation (e.g., where the sum of the managers' benchmarks is equal to something other than the benchmark index).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Macro evaluates the sponsor's deviations from strategic allocation (including gaps); micro evaluates the manager within the mandate.

options.A: provides good starting point for a conversation with clients, explaining both positive and negative aspects of recent performance.
options.B: Return attribution provides no benefit for client conversations.
options.C: Attribution serves no purpose when performance has been weak.

rationales.A:
Full statement: provides good starting point for a conversation with clients, explaining both positive and negative aspects of recent performance; return attribution analysis is particularly important when performance is weak; portfolio managers must demonstrate understanding of their performance, provide rationale for their decisions, and generate confidence in their ability to add value in the future.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Performance attribution provides a good starting point for a conversation with clients, explaining both positive and negative aspects of recent performance. Return attribution analysis is particularly important when performance is weak; portfolio managers must demonstrate an understanding of their performance, provide a rationale for their decisions, and generate confidence in their ability to add value in the future.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Attribution is the natural STARTING POINT for explaining results to clients.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Weak periods are where attribution earns its keep — explaining the negative alongside the positive.

options.A: Returns-based attribution requires full holdings and transaction data for every period.
options.B: Returns-based attribution is the most accurate of the three attribution approaches.
options.C: uses only the total portfolio returns over a period to identify the components of the investment process that have generated the returns.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Data-heavy requirements belong to holdings- and transactions-based approaches; returns-based needs only TOTAL RETURNS.
rationales.B: Incorrect. It is the LEAST accurate — the price of its minimal data appetite.
rationales.C:
Full statement: uses only the total portfolio returns over a period to identify the components of the investment process that have generated the returns; most appropriate when underlying portfolio holding information is not available with sufficient frequency at required level of detail (e.g., hedge funds because can be difficult to obtain underlying holdings); easiest method to implement but because it does not use underlying holdings, it is the least accurate of the three approaches and the most vulnerable to data manipulation.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Returns-based attribution uses only the total portfolio returns over a period to identify the components of the investment process that have generated the returns. Returns-based attribution is most appropriate when the underlying portfolio holding information is not available with sufficient frequency at the required level of detail. For example, one might use returns-based attribution to evaluate hedge funds, because it can be difficult to obtain the underlying holdings of hedge funds. Returns-based attribution is the easiest method to implement, but because it does not use the underlying holdings, it is the least accurate of the three approaches and the most vulnerable to data manipulation.'

options.A: Transactions-based attribution uses only period-end holdings snapshots.
options.B: uses both the holdings of the portfolio and the transactions (purchases and sales) that occurred during the evaluation period.
options.C: Transactions-based attribution uses only total portfolio returns.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Holdings snapshots alone define HOLDINGS-based attribution; adding purchases and sales is what upgrades it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: uses both the holdings of the portfolio and the transactions (purchases and sales) that occurred during the evaluation period; weights and returns reflect all transactions during period including transaction costs; most accurate type of attribution analysis but also most difficult and time-consuming to implement; underlying data must be complete, accurate, and reconciled from period to period; entire excess return can be quantified and explained; return will reconcile with return presented to client; can be used as diagnostic tool to identify errors.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The third approach, transactions-based attribution, uses both the holdings of the portfolio and the transactions (purchases and sales) that occurred during the evaluation period. For transaction-based attribution, both the weights and returns reflect all transactions during the period, including transaction costs. Transaction-based attribution is the most accurate type of attribution analysis but also the most difficult and time-consuming to implement. To obtain meaningful results, the underlying data must be complete, accurate, and reconciled from period to period. Because all the data are available, the entire excess return can be quantified and explained. The return used in the attribution analysis will reconcile with the return presented to the client, and attribution analysis can be used as a diagnostic tool to identify errors.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Returns-only is the returns-based method; transactions-based is the most complete and most accurate.

options.A: availability and quality of the underlying data, the reporting requirements for the client, and the complexity of the investment decision-making process.
options.B: The choice of attribution approach follows no identifiable criteria.
options.C: The attribution approach is dictated solely by the evaluator's software vendor.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'Performance attribution may be either returns based, holdings based, or transactions based. The decision to use one set of inputs rather than another depends on the availability of data as well as the investment process being measured.' Also: 'The choice of attribution approach depends on the availability and quality of the underlying data, the reporting requirements for the client, and the complexity of the investment decision-making process.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Three criteria govern: data availability and quality, client reporting requirements, and process complexity.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Vendor tools implement the choice; the criteria drive it.

options.A: all overweight positions in sectors with positive returns will generate positive allocation effects irrespective of overall benchmark return.
options.B: The BHB model has no known problems in allocation attribution.
options.C: The BHB model's problem is that it cannot compute allocation effects at all.

rationales.A:
Full statement: all overweight positions in sectors with positive returns will generate positive allocation effects irrespective of overall benchmark return; overweighting sector i that earns positive return B_i > 0 results in positive allocation A_i = (w_i - W_i) × B_i > 0 even when sector return is less than overall benchmark return (B_i < B); when sector return is negative, overweighting produces negative allocation; but if portfolio manager is overweight in negative market that has outperformed overall benchmark, effect should be positive — this is the BHB flaw.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the BHB model, all overweight positions in sectors with positive returns will generate positive allocation effects irrespective of the overall benchmark return, whereas all overweight positions in negative markets will generate negative allocation effects. Thus, overweighting a sector i that earns a positive return, B_i > 0, results in a positive allocation effect, A_i = (w_i − W_i)B_i > 0, even when the sector return is less than the overall benchmark return (i.e., B_i < B). When the sector return is negative, 0 > B_i, overweighting produces a negative allocation effect, A_i = (w_i − W_i)B_i < 0. Clearly, if the portfolio manager is overweight in a negative market that has outperformed the overall benchmark, the effect should be positive.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. A well-documented sign problem exists.
rationales.C: Incorrect. It computes them — but any overweight to a positive-return sector scores positive regardless of whether the sector BEAT the overall benchmark.

options.A: The Brinson-Fachler model makes no modification to the BHB allocation factor.
options.B: solves BHB problem by modifying asset allocation factor to compare returns with overall benchmark: A_i = (w_i - W_i) × (B_i - B).
options.C: Brinson-Fachler and BHB are identical models published under different names.

rationales.A: Incorrect. BF modifies the allocation term deliberately.
rationales.B:
Full statement: solves BHB problem by modifying asset allocation factor to compare returns with overall benchmark: A_i = (w_i - W_i) × (B_i - B); allocation effect at portfolio level (BS - B) unchanged from BHB model; applied to Exhibit 2 data: Energy 0.0%, Health care (30%-20%)×(-2%-8.2%) = -1.02%, Financials (20%-30%)×(12%-8.2%) = -0.38%; total still -1.4% at portfolio level but individual sector effects change.

Correct. Per Equation 10: 'The contribution to asset allocation in the ith sector is now: A_i = (w_i − W_i)(B_i − B). Note that in Equation 10, the allocation effect at the portfolio level, BS − B, is unchanged from the BHB model.' Data: 'Revised BF sector allocation effects are calculated for the portfolio data in Exhibit 2 as follows: Energy: (50% − 50%) × (10% − 8.2%) = 0.0%; Health care: (30% − 20%) × (−2.0% − 8.2%) = −1.02%; Financials: (20% − 30%) × (12% − 8.2%) = −0.38%; Total: 0.0% − 1.02% − 0.38% = −1.4%.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. BF compares sector returns against the OVERALL benchmark — A_i = (w_i − W_i)(B_i − B) — fixing BHB's sign inconsistency.

options.A: instead of estimating price changes from changes in duration and yields to maturity, bonds repriced from zero-coupon curves (spot rates).
options.B: No full-repricing method exists for yield curve decomposition.
options.C: Yield curve decomposition can only be approximated through duration times yield change.

rationales.A:
Full statement: instead of estimating price changes from changes in duration and yields to maturity, bonds repriced from zero-coupon curves (spot rates); more precise pricing and allows for broader range of instrument types and yield changes; supports greater variety of quantitative modeling beyond fixed-income attribution (e.g., ex ante risk); better aligned with how portfolio managers typically view instruments; requires full capability to reprice all financial instruments in portfolio and benchmark; complex nature can make it more difficult and costly to administer operationally and results more difficult to understand particularly for non-fixed-income professionals.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Instead of estimating price changes from changes in duration and yields to maturity, bonds can be repriced from zero-coupon curves (spot rates)... This full repricing attribution approach provides more precise pricing and allows for a broader range of instrument types and yield changes. It also supports a greater variety of quantitative modeling beyond fixed-income attribution (e.g., ex ante risk). This approach is better aligned with how portfolio managers typically view the instruments. However, it requires the full capability to reprice all financial instruments in the portfolio and the benchmark, including the rates and the characteristics of the instrument. Its complex nature can make it more difficult and costly to administer operationally and can make the results more difficult to understand, particularly for non-fixed-income professionals.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Full repricing from spot curves is the most precise method described.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Duration approximation is the simpler alternative; repricing bonds from zero-coupon curves is more exact, at higher complexity.

options.A: Broad market indexes and style indexes are the same construction under different labels.
options.B: No distinction exists between broad market and style benchmarks.
options.C: broad market indexes are measures of broad asset class performance (JP Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index EMBI for emerging market bonds, MSCI World Index for global developed market equities) — well known, readily available, easily understood.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Coverage differs — full asset class versus a style slice of it.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Broad indexes (e.g., EMBI for emerging market debt) measure the asset class; style indexes isolate segments like value or growth.
rationales.C:
Full statement: broad market indexes are measures of broad asset class performance (JP Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index EMBI for emerging market bonds, MSCI World Index for global developed market equities) — well known, readily available, easily understood; style indexes represent investment styles within asset classes; investment style is natural grouping of investment disciplines that has predictive power in explaining future dispersion of returns across portfolios (Brown and Goetzmann 1997); in late 1970s researchers found that stock valuation (P/E) and market cap explained much of stock return variation, leading to style versions of broad indexes (Russell 2000 Value, Russell 1000 Growth).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Broad market indexes are measures of broad asset class performance, such as the JP Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index (EMBI) for emerging market bonds or the MSCI World Index for global developed market equities. Broad market indexes are well known, readily available, and easily understood. The performance of broad market indexes is widely reported in the popular media. Market indexes have also been more narrowly defined to represent investment styles within asset classes, resulting in style indexes. An investment style is a natural grouping of investment disciplines that has some predictive power in explaining the future dispersion of returns across portfolios. In the late 1970s, researchers found that stock valuation (e.g., the price-to-earnings ratio) and market capitalization explained much of stock return variation. In response, many index providers created various style versions of their broad market indexes (e.g., the Russell 2000 Value and Russell 1000 Growth Indexes).'

options.A: No statistical tests exist for evaluating benchmark quality.
options.B: correlation between active management return A = (P - B) and style return S = (B - M) — a good benchmark should not reflect systematic biases.
options.C: Benchmark quality is evaluated only through qualitative committee judgment.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Correlation-based tests are standard.
rationales.B:
Full statement: correlation between active management return A = (P - B) and style return S = (B - M) — a good benchmark should not reflect systematic biases; correlation between A and S should not be statistically different from zero; define E = (P - M) as difference between portfolio and broad market index; when manager's style (S) is in (out of) favor relative to market, expect both benchmark and account to outperform (underperform) the market; a good benchmark will have a statistically significant positive correlation coefficient between S and E.

Correct. Per the reading: 'We can use these benchmark building blocks to further search for systematic biases between the active management return and the style return, identified through correlation. For instance, if we measure the correlation between active management return, A = (P − B), and style return, S = (B − M), we can identify whether the manager's active selection decisions align with the style currently favored by the market. A good benchmark should not reflect these systematic biases, where the correlation between A and S should not be statistically different from zero. Likewise, we define the difference between the portfolio and the broad market index as E = (P − M). When a manager's style (S) is in (out of ) favor relative to the market, we expect both the benchmark and the account to outperform (underperform) the market. Therefore, a good benchmark will have a statistically significant positive correlation coefficient between S and E.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The quantitative test correlates active return A = (P − B) with style return S = (B − M); a good benchmark leaves them uncorrelated.

options.A: benchmark meets criteria of unambiguous (individual securities and weights clearly identifiable), investable (shares freely tradeable), measurable (index returns published daily), reflective of current investment opinions (selected by manager), and specified in advance (not created after fact).
options.B: The JEMstone assessment finds the MSCI ACWI Small Cap benchmark meets every validity criterion without exception.
options.C: The assessment finds the benchmark meets none of the validity criteria.

rationales.A:
Full statement: benchmark meets criteria of unambiguous (individual securities and weights clearly identifiable), investable (shares freely tradeable), measurable (index returns published daily), reflective of current investment opinions (selected by manager), and specified in advance (not created after fact); does NOT meet criterion of appropriate — liquidity and capitalization constraints imposed by client are not consistent with manner in which manager runs other portfolios managed by firm; manager may choose to be accountable if liquidity/capitalization constraints not expected to interfere with strategy execution.

Correct. Per Example 10 solution: 'The benchmark meets the criteria of unambiguous. The individual securities and their weights are clearly identifiable. The benchmark most likely meets the criteria of investable. The shares in the index are freely tradeable. The benchmark meets the criteria of measurable. Index returns are published daily. The benchmark does not meet the criteria of appropriate. The liquidity and capitalization constraints imposed by the client are not consistent with the manner in which the manager runs other portfolios managed by the firm. The benchmark meets the criteria of reflective of current investment opinions. The benchmark was selected by the manager and is presumed to be representative of the manager's investment process. The benchmark meets the criteria of specified in advance. The benchmark is not created after the fact. The manager may choose to be accountable to this index if the liquidity and capitalization constraints are not expected to interfere with the ability to execute the investment strategy.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The APPROPRIATENESS criterion fails — the benchmark does not match the manager's investment process.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Most criteria pass: unambiguous, investable, measurable, specified in advance.

options.A: 'normal portfolio' or 'normal benchmark' is portfolio that most closely represents manager's typical positions in investment universe.
options.B: The normal portfolio concept has no definition in performance evaluation.
options.C: A normal portfolio is the market-cap-weighted index of the manager's home country.

rationales.A:
Full statement: 'normal portfolio' or 'normal benchmark' is portfolio that most closely represents manager's typical positions in investment universe; manager's 'true' active return equals return minus normal portfolio return; most investors tend to use broad market benchmark for manager evaluation, so manager's active return measured as manager's return minus investor's benchmark return; mismatch between broad market benchmark and manager's 'normal' portfolio is not manager's 'true' active return but 'misfit active return'; using broad market index typically misses manager's style (creates style bias).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Benchmark misspecification can lead to mismeasurement of the value added by the portfolio managers. A "normal portfolio" or "normal benchmark" is the portfolio that most closely represents the manager's typical positions in his investment universe. The manager's "true" active return is equal to his return minus his normal portfolio return. Most investors, however, tend to use a broad market benchmark for manager evaluation. The manager's active return is thus measured as the manager's return minus the investor's benchmark return. There is a mismatch between the broad market benchmark and the manager's "normal" portfolio or benchmark; this is not the manager's "true" active return but is more appropriately termed the "misfit active return" (see, e.g., Gastineau, Olma, and Zielinski 2007). Using a broad market index typically misses the manager's style (i.e., creates style bias).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The concept is defined and used for custom benchmarking.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The normal portfolio represents the manager's TYPICAL positions in the manager's own universe — not a country index.

options.A: Benchmarking alternative investments presents no distinctive challenges, since hedge fund and private equity index providers now publish investable benchmarks equivalent in quality to public equity indexes.
options.B: No framework identifies the difficulties in alternative-asset benchmarking, leaving evaluators to improvise on a case-by-case basis without recognized guidance.
options.C: selection of appropriate benchmark stymied by lack of high-quality investible market indexes, frequent use of leverage in many strategies, limited liquidity and lack of readily available market values for many underlying assets, and use of internal rates of return rather than time-weighted rates of return.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The challenges are structural.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The framework names them: scarce high-quality investable indexes, leverage, valuation lags, and heterogeneous strategies.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'Performance evaluation for alternative asset classes presents many challenges. The selection of an appropriate benchmark is stymied by the lack of high-quality, investible market indexes, the frequent use of leverage in many strategies, the limited liquidity and lack of readily available market values for many underlying assets, and the use of internal rates of return rather than time-weighted rates of return.'

options.A: hedge funds encompass broad range of possible strategies, may have unlimited investment universe, use leverage, sell assets short, take positions in derivatives, opportunistic in choice of strategy.
options.B: Hedge funds are benchmarked cleanly with a single standard index that captures every strategy from global macro to convertible arbitrage.
options.C: No framework addresses hedge fund benchmarking, since the industry's opacity makes systematic evaluation impossible.

rationales.A:
Full statement: hedge funds encompass broad range of possible strategies, may have unlimited investment universe, use leverage, sell assets short, take positions in derivatives, opportunistic in choice of strategy; broad market indexes are unsuitable as hedge fund benchmarks; risk-free rate (Treasury yield) plus spread (3%-6%) sometimes advocated for arbitrage strategies; both broad market indexes and risk-free rate will be weakly correlated or uncorrelated with hedge fund returns failing benchmark quality test; hedge fund manager universes from CSFB/Tremont often used but subject to survivorship bias and other limitations.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Hedge funds do not represent an asset class... These characteristics make it difficult to create a single standard against which hedge funds should be judged... These characteristics of hedge funds make it clear that broad market indexes are unsuitable as hedge fund benchmarks. The risk-free rate (e.g., Treasury yield) plus a spread (e.g., 3%–6%) is sometimes advocated as a hedge fund benchmark for arbitrage-based hedge fund strategies... Both broad market indexes and the risk-free rate will be weakly correlated or uncorrelated with hedge fund returns, thus failing the benchmark quality test of Bailey et al. (2007) that states that portfolio and benchmark factor sensitivities should be similar. Because of the shortcomings of broad market indexes and the risk-free rate, hedge fund manager universes from such providers as CSFB/Tremont are often used as hedge fund benchmarks.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. No single index fits — unlimited universes, leverage, and shorting defeat standard benchmarks.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The framework covers the difficulties directly, including why peer groups and factor models step in.

options.A: No Public Market Equivalent methodology exists for private equity evaluation.
options.B: uses cash flow data to replicate the general partner's capital calls and distributions, assuming these same cash flows were invested in the chosen equity index.
options.C: PME methodologies ignore fund cash flows and use only reported NAVs.

rationales.A: Incorrect. PME is the standard private-equity benchmarking family.
rationales.B:
Full statement: uses cash flow data to replicate the general partner's capital calls and distributions, assuming these same cash flows were invested in the chosen equity index; comparing performance of PME index with net IRR of fund reveals extent of over- or underperformance of PME index relative to public index; several PME methodologies exist, most common being Long-Nickels PME, PME+, Kaplan and Schoar PME, and Direct Alpha PME; important to choose appropriate PME for each private equity fund — poorly chosen PME raises risk of leading investor to incorrect conclusion.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The methodology uses cash flow data to replicate the general partner's capital calls and distributions, assuming these same cash flows were invested in the chosen equity index. Comparing the performance of the PME index with the net IRR of the fund reveals the extent of over- or underperformance of the PME index relative to the public index. Several PME methodologies exist, the most common being Long–Nickels PME, PME+, Kaplan and Schoar PME, and Direct Alpha PME. It is important to choose the appropriate PME for each private equity fund; a poorly chosen PME raises the risk of leading the investor to an incorrect conclusion.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Cash flows are the INPUT — capital calls and distributions replicated as if invested in the public index.

options.A: No framework exists for benchmarking commodity investments.
options.B: Commodity benchmarking presents no problems because spot indexes are directly investable.
options.C: commodity benchmarks tend to use indexes based on performance of futures-based commodity investments (Reuters/Jefferies CRB Index, S&P Goldman Sachs Commodity Index GSCI, Bloomberg Commodity Index BCOM).

rationales.A: Incorrect. The framework exists, built on futures-based indexes.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Spot indexes are NOT directly investable — hence futures-based benchmarks (CRB, S&P GSCI) with their roll and collateral return complications.
rationales.C:
Full statement: commodity benchmarks tend to use indexes based on performance of futures-based commodity investments (Reuters/Jefferies CRB Index, S&P Goldman Sachs Commodity Index GSCI, Bloomberg Commodity Index BCOM); indexes use futures rather than actual assets; because cost-of-carry model ensures return on fully margined position in futures contract mimics return on underlying spot deliverable, futures contract returns often used as surrogate for cash market performance; challenges include use of derivatives to represent actual commodity assets, varying degrees of leverage among funds, discretionary weighting of exposures; market-cap-weighting cannot be carried over to commodity indexes because every long futures position has corresponding short futures position — market cap of futures contract is always zero.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Commodity benchmarks tend to use indexes based on the performance of futures-based commodity investments. These include the Reuters/Jefferies Commodity Research Bureau (RJ/CRB) Index, the S&P Goldman Sachs Commodity Index (GSCI), and the Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM). However, because the indexes use futures, rather than actual assets, they attempt to replicate the returns available to holding long positions in commodities... A market-cap-weighting scheme, so common for equity and bond market indexes, cannot be carried over to indexes of commodity futures. Because every long futures position has a corresponding short futures position, the market capitalization of a futures contract is always zero. Benchmarking of commodity investments presents similar challenges to other alternatives, including the use of derivatives to represent actual commodity assets, varying degrees of leverage among funds, and the discretionary weighting of exposures within the index.'

options.A: The Sharpe ratio has no recognized weaknesses as a performance measure.
options.B: the use of standard deviation as a measure of risk assumes investors are indifferent between upside and downside volatility.
options.C: The Sharpe ratio's weakness is that it ignores risk entirely.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A well-known weakness exists in its risk measure.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the use of standard deviation as a measure of risk assumes investors are indifferent between upside and downside volatility; for investor looking for potentially high-rewarding investment, volatility on upside is not necessarily a negative; risk-averse investors concerned about preservation of capital are most concerned with downside risk; Sortino ratio addresses this by using downside deviation.

Correct. Per the reading: 'One weakness of the Sharpe ratio is that the use of standard deviation as a measure of risk assumes investors are indifferent between upside and downside volatility. For example, for an investor looking for a potentially high-rewarding investment, volatility on the upside is not necessarily a negative. Similarly, risk-averse investors concerned about the preservation of capital are clearly most concerned with downside risk.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Risk is central to the ratio — the flaw is that standard deviation treats UPSIDE and downside volatility identically.

options.A: Distinguishing manager skill from luck presents no difficulty for evaluators.
options.B: Skill-versus-luck evaluation is not a recognized challenge in the literature.
options.C: investment manager's record for any specific period will reflect good luck (unanticipated good developments) and bad luck (unanticipated bad developments).

rationales.A: Incorrect. The difficulty is fundamental.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The challenge is canonical — any period's record blends skill with unanticipated good and bad developments.
rationales.C:
Full statement: investment manager's record for any specific period will reflect good luck (unanticipated good developments) and bad luck (unanticipated bad developments); paradox of skill — as people become more knowledgeable about an activity, difference between worst and best performers becomes narrower; ever-increasing aggregate skill level of investment managers may lead to narrower performance differentials and greater likelihood that differentials can be explained by luck; financial market returns have large element of randomness; hypothesis that manager's underlying mean return exceeds benchmark may require many years of observations to confirm with reasonably high degree of confidence.

Correct. Per the reading: 'An investment manager's record for any specific period will reflect good luck (unanticipated good developments) and bad luck (unanticipated bad developments). One reason that luck should be considered important when appraising investment performance is the paradox of skill. As people become more knowledgeable about an activity, the difference between the worst and the best performers becomes narrower. Thus, the ever-increasing aggregate skill level of investment managers, supplemented by massive computing power and access to "big data," may lead to narrower investment performance differentials and a greater likelihood that these differentials can be explained by luck. Deciding whether a portfolio manager has or lacks active investment skill on the basis of past returns is difficult and always subject to error. Financial market returns have a large element of randomness... the hypothesis that the manager's underlying mean return exceeds the benchmark's mean return may require many years of observations to confirm with a reasonably high degree of confidence.'

options.A: No framework guides the evaluation of investment manager skill.
options.B: A single appraisal ratio suffices to establish manager skill conclusively.
options.C: evaluation of investment manager skill requires use of broad range of analysis tools with fundamental understanding of how tools work, how they complement each other, and their specific limitations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The framework prescribes combining tools.
rationales.B: Incorrect. No single measure settles it — a broad toolkit applied with understanding of each tool's construction and limits is required.
rationales.C:
Full statement: evaluation of investment manager skill requires use of broad range of analysis tools with fundamental understanding of how tools work, how they complement each other, and their specific limitations; appraisal ratios must be used with care noting assumptions of each ratio and affording appropriateness to measured investment process, risk tolerance, and investor time horizon; although appraisal ratios help identify manager skill (as opposed to luck), often based on investment return data which are often limited and subject to error.

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'Investment performance appraisal ratios — including the Sortino ratio, upside/downside capture ratios, maximum drawdown, and drawdown duration — measure investment skill. Appraisal ratios must be used with care, noting the assumptions of each ratio and affording the appropriateness to the measured investment process, risk tolerance, and investor time horizon. Although appraisal ratios help identify manager skill (as opposed to luck), they often are based on investment return data, which are often limited and subject to error. Evaluation of investment manager skill requires the use of a broad range of analysis tools, with fundamental understanding of how the tools work, how they complement each other, and their specific limitations.'

options.A: For inflation protection, the reading recommends concentrating in long-duration nominal bonds.
options.B: For inflation protection, the reading recommends holding only cash equivalents.
options.C: companies that can successfully pass on higher input costs to customers, or commodity producers that may benefit from rising commodity prices.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Long-duration nominal bonds are among the assets MOST damaged by inflation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Cash loses purchasing power in inflation; pricing-power companies and commodity producers are the equity answer.
rationales.C:
Full statement: companies that can successfully pass on higher input costs to customers, or commodity producers that may benefit from rising commodity prices — recognizing that equities' inflation-hedge efficacy is mixed at best.

Correct. The reading's inflation-hedge discussion (and the Huber portfolio-manager example) notes: companies successful at passing along higher input costs (raw materials, energy, wages) to customers can protect margins; commodity producers (oil, industrial metals) benefit directly from commodity price rises. Certain sectors provide some protection though the asset class overall is mixed as an inflation hedge.

options.A: For the Chinese robotics fund, geographic segmentation alone is the recommended framework.
options.B: size/style segmentation with growth-oriented style (size depending on the company), since the fund's rapid-earnings-growth focus best aligns with growth style.
options.C: For the fund, economic activity segmentation alone captures the relevant characteristics.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Geography is already fixed by the mandate — it adds no discriminating power.
rationales.B:
Full statement: size/style segmentation with growth-oriented style (size depending on the company), since the fund's rapid-earnings-growth focus best aligns with growth style; geography and economic activity segmentation are less appropriate given the similar geographic and industry composition of the target industry.

Correct. Per the reading's example, based on the desired strategy to invest in companies with rapidly accelerating earnings, the manager would most likely segment by size/style — using an investment style that reflects growth, with size (large, mid, or small cap) depending on the company. Other segmentation approaches (geography and economic activity) would be less appropriate given the similar geographic and industry composition of the Chinese robotics industry.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Sector segmentation misses the accelerating-earnings dimension; size/style with a GROWTH orientation fits the fund's character.

options.A: The reading's example of a multi-approach index combination is the S&P 500 alone.
options.B: The reading's example is the Dow Jones Industrial Average.
options.C: the MSCI Europe Large Cap Growth Index and the MSCI World Small Cap Value Index, which combine geographic, size, and style dimensions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The S&P 500 reflects a single approach — selective large-cap US.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The DJIA is a 30-stock price-weighted index; the cited combinations are MSCI Europe Large Cap Growth and MSCI World Small Cap Value, blending geography, size, and style.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading identifies these indexes as examples combining geography, size, and style: MSCI Europe Large Cap Growth Index, MSCI World Small Cap Value Index, MSCI Emerging Markets Large Cap Growth Index, and MSCI Latin America Midcap Index. This combination also sometimes applies to individual countries, particularly large developed markets.

options.A: the S&P Global Natural Resources Index (90 largest natural resources/commodities companies across agribusiness, energy, and metals and mining) and the MSCI World Energy Index (large-cap and mid-cap oil and natural gas).
options.B: Sector-based equity indexes are constructed only within single countries.
options.C: Multi-country indexes exist only for whole markets, never for individual sectors.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading cites these as examples of sector indexes that are global in scope: S&P Global Natural Resources Index (90 largest publicly traded companies in natural resources/commodities across agribusiness, energy, and metals and mining); MSCI World Energy Index (large-cap and mid-cap segments of publicly traded oil and natural gas in developed markets); Refinitiv Global Financials Index (100 largest global financial services). Because many sectors are global, industry-focused indexes are often global.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Sector indexes routinely cross borders.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The S&P Global Natural Resources Index — 90 companies spanning agribusiness, energy, and metals worldwide — is the cited example.

options.A: In size/style segmentation, style is determined by market capitalization alone.
options.B: a scoring system incorporating multiple metrics or ratios such as price-to-book, price-to-earnings, earnings growth, dividend yield, and book value growth.
options.C: Style is determined by the geographic domicile of the company.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Capitalization defines the SIZE axis; style needs valuation and growth metrics.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a scoring system incorporating multiple metrics or ratios such as price-to-book, price-to-earnings, earnings growth, dividend yield, and book value growth — scored individually, weighted, and aggregated to produce a composite score positioning the stock along the value-growth spectrum.

Correct. The reading states style is often determined through a scoring system that incorporates multiple metrics or ratios (P/B, P/E, earnings growth, dividend yield, book value growth). These metrics are scored individually for each company, assigned weights, and aggregated to produce a composite score determining where the stock falls along the value-growth spectrum. A combination of growth and value is not uncommon, particularly for large corporations with both mature and higher-growth business lines.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Domicile is the geographic approach; style scoring blends price-to-book, price-to-earnings, earnings growth, and dividend yield.

options.A: predatory trading — where predatory traders purchase (or sell short) shares prior to their effective inclusion (or deletion) from an index, resulting in price movement and profit for the predatory trader at the expense of the index fund.
options.B: Index funds face no hidden costs given their transparency and low turnover.
options.C: The hidden cost identified is that index funds pay higher commissions than active funds.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading states index funds face a 'hidden' cost from potential predatory trading. A predatory trader may purchase (or sell short) shares prior to their effective inclusion (or deletion) from an index, resulting in price movement and potential profit for the predatory trader. Such strategies can be regarded as a cost to index-fund investors — albeit a cost that is not necessarily evident to a portfolio manager or investor.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Transparency is the vulnerability — predictable trades invite predation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Commissions are LOWER for index funds; the hidden cost is predatory trading ahead of announced inclusions and deletions.

options.A: Momentum and deep value strategies have identical trading cost profiles, since both trade the same universe of listed equities through the same brokers and venues.
options.B: momentum strategies 'demand liquidity' (buying stocks already rising, selling those already falling) so tend to have higher trading costs, while deep value strategies more likely 'provide liquidity' to the market (buying significantly undervalued stocks) so tend to have lower trading costs.
options.C: Deep value strategies always incur higher trading costs than momentum strategies, because unloved stocks trade with wider spreads that dominate any liquidity-demand effect.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Their liquidity postures differ fundamentally.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states some active investing approaches 'demand liquidity' from the market — for example, in a momentum strategy the investor seeks to buy shares already rising in price (or sell those already falling). Deep value strategies (involving stocks deemed significantly undervalued) are more likely to 'provide liquidity' to the market. Investment strategies that involve frequent trading and demand liquidity are, unsurprisingly, likely to have higher trading costs than long-term, buy-and-hold investment strategies.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The relationship runs the other way — momentum DEMANDS liquidity (buying what's rising), while patient value buying tends to SUPPLY it.

options.A: Index funds lend stocks frequently because regulation requires them to do so.
options.B: of their large, long-term holdings in stocks, and because index-fund managers are normally not concerned that borrowed stock used for short-selling might depress the corresponding equity prices.
options.C: Index funds lend stocks because they need external cash to fund operations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. No lending mandate exists; the practice is commercial.
rationales.B:
Full statement: of their large, long-term holdings in stocks, and because index-fund managers are normally not concerned that borrowed stock used for short-selling might depress the corresponding equity prices — index funds merely seek to replicate benchmark performance.

Correct. The reading states index funds are frequent stock lenders because of their large, long-term holdings in stocks. In addition, because index funds merely seek to replicate the performance of an index, portfolio managers of these funds are normally not concerned that borrowed stock used for short-selling purposes might decrease the prices of the corresponding equities. Large actively managed pension funds, endowments, and institutional investors are also frequent lenders (though more concerned with short-selling impact).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Operational cash is not the driver — large, stable long-term holdings and indifference to short-sale price pressure make lending attractive income.

options.A: Shareholder engagement is defined as purely passive index investing without interaction.
options.B: Shareholder engagement means the buying and selling of shares in secondary markets.
options.C: the process whereby investors actively interact with companies, including voting on corporate matters at general meetings as well as other forms of communication (quarterly investor calls, in-person meetings).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Passivity in trading does not preclude — and is not — engagement.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Trading is portfolio management; engagement is ACTIVE INTERACTION with companies, including voting at general meetings.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the process whereby investors actively interact with companies, including voting on corporate matters at general meetings as well as other forms of communication (quarterly investor calls, in-person meetings) — generally concerning issues that can affect the value of a company and, by extension, an investor's shares.

Correct. The reading defines shareholder engagement as the process whereby investors actively interact with companies. It often includes voting on corporate matters at general meetings as well as other forms of communication (quarterly investor calls, in-person meetings). Generally, engagement concerns issues that can affect the value of a company and, by extension, an investor's shares.

options.A: Proxy voting is prohibited under most jurisdictions' corporate law.
options.B: Proxy voting refers to voting conducted via cryptocurrency tokens.
options.C: enabling shareholders unable to attend a meeting to authorize another individual (another shareholder or director) to vote on their behalf.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Proxy voting is standard, legally supported practice worldwide.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The proxy is a PERSON — another shareholder or director authorized to vote for a shareholder unable to attend.
rationales.C:
Full statement: enabling shareholders unable to attend a meeting to authorize another individual (another shareholder or director) to vote on their behalf — the most common form of investor participation in general meetings.

Correct. The reading defines proxy voting as enabling shareholders who are unable to attend a meeting to authorize another individual (e.g., another shareholder or director) to vote on their behalf. Proxy voting is the most common form of investor participation in general meetings. Although most resolutions pass without controversy, sometimes minority shareholders attempt to strengthen their influence via proxy voting. Multiple shareholders may collectively vote in favor of or in opposition to a resolution.

options.A: When shares are loaned, the voting rights remain with the lender at all times, since a securities loan transfers only economic exposure rather than legal title.
options.B: the transaction is technically an assignment of title with a repurchase option — voting rights are transferred to the borrower — which could potentially result in the borrower having different voting opinions from the lending investor.
options.C: When shares are loaned, the voting rights are permanently extinguished for the duration of the loan, with neither the lender nor the borrower entitled to vote them.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Title transfers with the loan — and voting rights travel with title to the borrower.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the transaction is technically an assignment of title with a repurchase option — voting rights are transferred to the borrower — which could potentially result in the borrower having different voting opinions from the lending investor; some stock lenders therefore recall shares ahead of voting resolutions.

Correct. The reading states when an investor loans shares, the transaction is technically an assignment of title with a repurchase option — voting rights are transferred to the borrower. The transfer could potentially result in the borrower having different voting opinions from the lending investor. To mitigate this, some stock lenders recall shares ahead of voting resolutions. The downside is loss of stock lending revenue during the recall period and potential reputation risk. In some cases, investors borrow shares explicitly to exercise voting rights ('empty voting') — no capital invested but voting rights acquired.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Rights transfer, not vanish; lenders wanting to vote must recall shares before the record date.

options.A: From the company's perspective, shareholder engagement always harms performance by distracting management.
options.B: can assist in developing a more effective corporate governance culture, which may lead to better company performance to the benefit of shareholders and other stakeholders.
options.C: Engagement eliminates all disagreements between companies and their shareholders.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The reading identifies BENEFITS — engagement can strengthen governance culture.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states from a company's perspective, shareholder engagement can assist in developing a more effective corporate governance culture. In turn, shareholder engagement may lead to better company performance to the benefit of shareholders as well as other stakeholders. Investors also benefit — through more information about companies or the sectors in which they operate.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disagreement persists; the benefit is a more effective governance culture that can improve performance for all shareholders.

options.A: Activist investing is purely passive index tracking with no engagement component.
options.B: Activist investing is always and only focused on management compensation.
options.C: a distinct and specialized version of engagement — activists specialize in taking stakes in companies and creating change to generate a gain on the investment.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Activism is engagement INTENSIFIED, not passivity.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Compensation is one topic among many; activists take stakes to create change that generates investment gains.
rationales.C:
Full statement: a distinct and specialized version of engagement — activists specialize in taking stakes in companies and creating change to generate a gain on the investment; hedge funds are among the most common activists due to potential high performance fees, limited regulation, fewer investment constraints, and ability to leverage positions.

Correct. The reading defines activist investing as a distinct and specialized version of engagement. Activists specialize in taking stakes in companies and creating change to generate a gain on their investment. Hedge funds are among the most common activists, possibly because of the potential for high performance fees. Additionally, because hedge funds are subject to limited regulation, have fewer investment constraints, and can often leverage positions, they often have more flexibility as activists.

options.A: For the cost-conscious efficiency-believing client, highly concentrated active management is the recommendation.
options.B: For this client, a long/short hedge fund structure is the recommendation.
options.C: index-based, because the client believes in market efficiency (implying alpha is difficult to earn), values low costs, and narrow country/sector focus is typically well-served by index-based products.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Belief in efficiency undercuts the case for concentrated active risk — and the costs contradict her priorities.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Long/short is high-cost active management; index-based exposure fits her efficiency view, cost sensitivity, and narrow focus.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading's Goudreaux example: cost consciousness and belief in market efficiency drive her toward index-based management. Because she believes in market efficiency, she likely believes it is challenging for active managers to outperform. Because she is cost conscious, index-based (typically lower fee) is attractive. Her narrow country/sector focus is well-served by index-based sector or country ETFs. So her portfolio is likely index-based.

options.A: Confidence to outperform requires no fundamental research capability on the manager's part.
options.B: an understanding of the manager's equity investment universe, a competitive analysis of other managers with a similar universe, and appropriate resources (research staff and access to information).
options.C: Confidence to outperform requires a contractual guarantee of alpha delivery.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Research capability is a required foundation.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states that active investment managers typically need to be confident they can adequately outperform their benchmark. This requires an understanding of the manager's equity investment universe, a competitive analysis of other managers with a similar universe, and appropriate resources (e.g., research staff and access to information). Without these, outperformance confidence is unfounded.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Alpha cannot be guaranteed; the requirement is universe understanding, competitive analysis of similar managers, and demonstrable insight.

options.A: Suitable benchmarks for active managers carry no liquidity requirement for underlying securities.
options.B: benchmarks with sufficient liquidity of underlying securities (thus maintaining a reasonable cost of trading), and enough underlying securities to generate sufficient alpha.
options.C: A single-stock benchmark is suitable for a diversified active mandate.
